Title: A phenol-enriched cuticle is ancestral to lignin evolution in land plants

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Phenylpropanoid/pre-lignin hydroxycinnamate pathway coupled to ascorbate-derived threonic acid metabolism; cuticle/cutin-associated phenolic biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point; entry point to moss pre-lignin pathway; caffeic acid production described as limiting factor for cuticle biopolymer biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Meta-hydroxylates the phenolic ring of p-coumaroyl-threonate esters to form corresponding caffeoyl-threonate esters
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: caffeic acid derivatives in cuticle/cutin (caffeate residues)
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes to cuticle formation, impermeability, desiccation protection, organ separation/fusion prevention, erect growth, and likely antioxidant/UV-screening properties
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes caffeoyl-threonate and cutin caffeate formation; required for normal gametophore development, erect growth, and cuticle impermeability; knockout causes organ fusion and aborted gametophores
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PpCYP98 is the single-copy CYP98 in P. patens and is highly expressed in developing aerial tissues. 2. Deletion abolishes caffeoyl-threonate production and eliminates free/bound caffeic acid formation. 3. Recombinant PpCYP98 converts p-coumaroyl-threonate esters into caffeoyl-threonate esters in vitro. 4. Mutants show defective cuticle, organ fusion, and failed gametophore development. 5. Exogenous caffeic acid partially rescues growth and cuticle impermeability, supporting caffeic acid as a limiting product downstream of PpCYP98.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Targeted gene deletion/knockout in moss; GUS reporter expression; LC-MS/MS metabolite profiling; acid hydrolysis of extracts; heterologous expression in yeast with in vitro enzyme assay; cutin monomer analysis by GC/GC-MS; toluidine blue permeability assay; transmission electron microscopy; chemical complementation with caffeic acid
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Mostly expressed in above-ground haploid gametophytic and diploid sporophytic tissues; highest in developing gametophores, newly emerged and elongating phyllids, reproductive organs, embryos, and developing sporophytes; low in protonema
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Mostly expressed in above-ground haploid gametophytic and diploid sporophytic tissues; highest in developing gametophores, newly emerged and elongating phyllids, reproductive organs, embryos, and developing sporophytes; low in protonema
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Arabidopsis thaliana ATR1 cytochrome P450 reductase in yeast assay system
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptome atlas (no accession reported); metabolite profiling by LC-MS/MS
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Exogenous caffeic acid supplementation (20 μM) for chemical complementation; standard growth under 16/8 h day/night cycle at 23 °C
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Knockout causes severe developmental defects, aborted gametophore formation, loss of sexual reproduction, organ fusion, and increased cuticle permeability
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for engineering phenol-enriched protective biopolymers and enhancing surface impermeability/stress tolerance in land plants; likely requires careful balancing because loss of function severely impairs development
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Unlike angiosperm CYP98 enzymes that use p-coumaroyl-shikimate, PpCYP98 uses p-coumaroyl-threonate, linking caffeic acid formation to ascorbate catabolism rather than shikimate coupling; enzyme did not hydroxylate palmitic acid in vitro
